persona.name: Hana Park

occupation.title: Part-time Sales Associate / Beauty Advisor
occupation.organization: Specialty beauty retailer (Sephora flagship in Los Angeles, part-time role; also occasionally covers shifts at an indie K-beauty boutique)
occupation.description: Works roughly 20–25 hours/week while attending university full-time. Primary responsibilities include greeting customers, guiding them to appropriate skincare categories (cleansers, toners, essences, serums, masks), demonstrating textures and layering techniques, providing mini skin consultations, applying sample testers, and maintaining product displays. Handles point-of-sale transactions, restocking samples, organizing product testing stations, and reporting customer feedback to brand reps. Regularly participates in new-launch trainings, reads brand sell-sheets, and helps run in-store events (tester nights, influencer meet-and-greets). Comfortable with upselling and meeting monthly sales targets. Skills include verbal product explanation, basic skin analysis, familiarity with active ingredients, merchandising, basic cash handling, scheduling, and simple conflict resolution with customers. Uses store CRM to note customer preferences and follow up on limited releases. Has learned to balance customer service with maintaining a quick checkout line during busy hours.

persona.residence: Los Angeles, California (urban, West Coast metropolitan neighborhood near Koreatown)
persona.education: Currently a junior at the University of California (transferred from community college). Pursuing a B.A. in Psychology with a minor in Marketing. Strong academic record (3.6 GPA). Completed coursework in developmental psychology, statistics for behavioral sciences, consumer behavior, and a seminar on media influence. Has taken a few continuing-education online classes about cosmetic formulation basics and an introductory course on social media analytics.
long_term_goals: Establish financial independence and be able to comfortably support herself and help family when needed.
Graduate with a BA in Psychology and either work in consumer research / market insights for beauty brands or continue to an M.S. in consumer psychology.
Build a small side business (e.g., curated beauty subscription box or micro-influencer channel) that supplements income.
Be seen as knowledgeable and trusted among peers about skincare and beauty — a go-to person for product recommendations.
Travel through East Asia (South Korea, Japan, Taiwan) to research beauty trends and visit factories/retail concepts.
Purchase a small condo in a walkable neighborhood in the next 8–12 years.
Create a balanced life that includes meaningful friendships, a steady career, and hobbies (photography, ceramics).
Learn Korean fluently and deepen connection to family cultural heritage.
Develop long-term healthy skin and body-care habits to prevent worsening hyperpigmentation.
Be comfortable in her body and cultivate self-confidence beyond external validation from likes or compliments.
persona.style: Warm, conversational, and slightly effusive when talking about beauty; speaks with a Midwestern-Los Angeles English accent with occasional Korean code-switching around family or when describing specific Korean products. Uses casual, trend-savvy vocabulary ('glass skin', 'dewy', 'slugging', 'brightening') and drops influencer slang ('HG' for holy grail, 'dupe', 'repurchase') when with friends. Mannerisms: speaks with expressive hand gestures, tilts head when listening, laughs softly and often, hums while testing product textures, taps her phone to cue videos, and bites the inside of her cheek when making quick decisions. Typically dresses in layered, practical street-style influenced outfits: oversized vintage jacket, clean sneakers, tote bag filled with samples. Uses polite, customer-friendly phrasing in retail settings — 'Have you tried...', 'This might be great for your concerns' — and is more candid with close friends. Prefers clear, concrete language over abstract theory when explaining routines. Often narrates her beauty routine like a step-by-step tutorial, and enjoys making playlists for morning vs. nighttime routines.

traits: Curious and experimental—actively seeks new products and routines to learn how ingredients work on her skin.
Socially warm—easy to start conversations and make people feel comfortable in retail settings.
Conscientious about school and work—meets deadlines, keeps a detailed planner, and tracks budgeting for purchases.
Trend-sensitive—quick to notice viral launches and social conversations around beauty.
Analytical—compares ingredient lists, reads study abstracts, and triangulates user reviews before committing to a mid- to high-price product.
Insecure about visible dark spots—sometimes overthinks photos and filters.
Resourceful—uses samples, returns, and sales strategically to try more products within budget constraints.
Playful—enjoys experimenting with bright lip tints and seasonal color trends.
Patient in routines—committed to multi-step regimens and willing to test a product for weeks before judging.
Resilient and pragmatic—bounces back from bad purchases and reframes them as learning experiences.
Loyal to brands that deliver results—willing to repurchase and recommend when something works.
Community-oriented—shares product finds with friends and online groups and values crowd knowledge.

big_five.openness: High. Loves novelty (new product technologies, international brands, cultural beauty rituals), curious about creative approaches to skincare and beauty.
big_five.conscientiousness: Medium-High. Organized with school and finances, keeps detailed notes on product testing, but indulges in impulse buys occasionally.
big_five.extraversion: Moderate. Enjoys social environments, connecting with customers and friends, but also values solo time for self-care and study.
big_five.agreeableness: High. Cooperative, friendly, prioritizes harmony, helpful in retail and among friends, sometimes avoids confrontational feedback.
big_five.neuroticism: Medium. Experiences worry about appearance and future finances; manages anxiety with routines, but has occasional stress spikes around exams or work shifts.

interests: Skincare: enthusiastic about multi-step routines (toner, essence, hydrating serums, targeted acids, moisturizers, sunscreen, sheet masks).
K-beauty trends: follows glass-skin, brightening, 'no-makeup' makeup, and layering essences.
YouTube long-form product reviews and routine tutorials (favorites: 20–30 minute deep dives and ingredient explainers).
Visiting specialty beauty retailers (Sephora, Ulta, local indie boutiques) to test textures and talk to brand reps.
Watching ingredient-focused creators who cite studies and clinical evidence.
Collecting samples—sheet masks, ampoules, deluxe-sized serums.
Photography: curating aesthetically pleasing flat-lay shots of products for her notes and occasional Instagram Stories.
Language learning: practicing Korean with family apps and watching K-beauty content in Korean with subtitles.
Budget-conscious shopping: waiting for VIB sales, holiday promos, and brand bundles.
Trying new cafes and boba shops with friends (prefers fruit tea or brown sugar milk tea with less sugar).
Fitness light: yoga classes twice weekly and weekend group hikes with friends.
Studying people: enjoys observing consumer behavior in-store and in online comment threads.
Journaling: keeps a small beauty journal logging product reactions and photos.
Sustainable packaging curiosity: notices recyclable packaging but prioritizes efficacy first.
Sheet masks on low-energy days and before big social events.
Seasonal switches: moves from heavier moisturizers in winter to lightweight gel creams in summer.
Preference for lightweight, fast-absorbing textures that layer well under makeup.
A soft spot for pastel packaging and minimalist brand design—buys or keeps packaging she finds pretty.
Occasional DIY face mask experiments at home (honey + plain yogurt or rice water soak).
Enjoys gifting curated mini skincare sets to close friends for birthdays.
likes: Vitamin C serums for brightening (prefers stable formulations in pump or opaque bottles).
Niacinamide for tone-evening and pore texture improvement.
Hyaluronic acid serums and lightweight essences for daily hydration.
Gentle exfoliating acids (PHA/AHA) for mild resurfacers rather than harsh scrubs.
Broad-spectrum sunscreen daily (chemical/physical depending on finish); prefers non-white-cast formulas for photo-friendly skin).
Sheet masks with hydrating ampoules for night care.
Sunscreen reapplication cushions for on-the-go touch ups.
Affordable 'drugstore-meets-K-beauty' launches that deliver visible results.
Small sample sizes to trial actives without committing to full-size.
Neutral-toned makeup that looks natural—'my skin but better' looks.
YouTube creators who do ingredient breakdowns and before/after comparisons.
In-store testers and deluxe samples from prestige brands.
Products with pleasing textures—silky serums, non-tacky moisturizers.
Minimalist packaging with a premium feel (glass bottles, pump dispensers).
Brands that publish clinical claims and explain study designs in plain language.
Late-afternoon walks with a friend and iced tea.
Comfort foods that remind her of home (korean japchae, mandu, or simple rice bowls).
Accessories like cute hair clips to keep hair away during sheet masks.
Seasonal, limited-edition scents and finishes (e.g., a soft peach mask).
Listening to curated Spotify playlists during long skincare sessions.
dislikes: Harsh drying cleansers or those that leave a tight, stripped feeling.
Products that make exaggerated marketing claims with no evidence.
Heavy, greasy creams that pill under makeup.
Brands that overuse netizen culture for marketing without substance.
Sunscreens that leave a visible white cast on Asian skin tones.
Pushy salespeople who pressure into purchases without product education.
Complicated return/exchange policies for sensitive skin reactions.
Perfumed skincare that irritates sensitive eyes or nose.
Single-use excessive plastic packaging and wasteful product luxury samples.
Virally hyped items that don't match her skin's needs despite influencer enthusiasm.
Products unavailable in her region or that have expensive international shipping.
Overly clinical or jargon-heavy descriptions without consumer translation.
Crowds during in-store launches that make testing impossible.
Trend-chasing products with no clear ingredient rationale.
Makeup that oxidizes significantly on her skin.
Being told to 'just moisturize more' when concerned about hyperpigmentation.
Brands that ignore Asian skin needs in shade ranges and sunscreen finishes.
Products that cause immediate stinging or burning sensations.
Messy, dripping sheet masks that make lying down uncomfortable.
Bubbles or sticky residues that get on clothing or pillowcases.
shopping_habits: Buys at least one new skincare product every month, often during sales or with store samples.
Sets a monthly skincare budget around $80, splitting between drugstore favorites and one prestige purchase each quarter.
Obsesses over ingredient lists and cross-checks pH levels and active concentrations when available.
Relies on in-store testers, deluxe samples, and 'patch test' trials for 1–2 weeks before committing to full-size.
Keeps a shared Google Sheet of products tried, reaction notes, and 'repurchase' flags.
Registers for brand newsletters to receive early access to limited drops and promo codes.
Prefers buying from specialty retailers for testers and immediate returns rather than buying blind online, except for international K-beauty exclusives.
Frequently reads 10–50 user reviews and watches a 15–30 minute YouTube review before a higher-ticket purchase.
Will buy viral items impulsively 1–2 times a year, but monitors results and returns if necessary.
Uses cashback apps or student discounts when available to stretch the budget.
media_consumption: YouTube long-form reviews and routines (70% of discovery for deep-dive info).
Instagram for quick visuals, saveable product posts, and Stories from favorite creators.
TikTok for short-format trend alerts and quick demos, but verifies on YouTube before purchasing.
Beauty blogs and Reddit threads (r/SkincareAddiction) for ingredient threads and community experiences.
Brand emails and in-store promotions for sales and events.
Occasional podcast episodes about entrepreneurship and beauty industry business models.
Video essays about Korean beauty history and how cultural practices inform modern products.

beliefs: Consistent sunscreen use is the single most important habit to prevent hyperpigmentation and premature aging.
Brightening products (vitamin C, niacinamide, mild acids) work best when used consistently over months, not days.
K-beauty innovations often emphasize hydration-first approaches which are beneficial for many skin types.
Influencer reviews are useful but should be corroborated with ingredient knowledge and clinical claims.
Skin tone diversity matters and many brands still need to improve formulations so they work well on Asian skin tones without white cast.
Samples and patch-testing are essential for reducing waste and avoiding unnecessary allergic reactions.
Education about product formulation empowers consumers to save money and avoid harmful trends.
Minimalist routines can be effective if they include hydration, sun protection, and targeted actives.
Expensive does not always equal better—some drugstore or mid-tier products outperform prestige items for certain actives.
Community knowledge-sharing (friends, forums) is a powerful tool for discovery and troubleshooting.
Transparency in labeling (ingredient concentrations, pH) should be standard practice.
Sunscreen reapplication is inconvenient but non-negotiable for brightening goals; convenient formats increase adherence.
Sustainability is important but a secondary factor to actual product efficacy when skin health is at stake.
Cruelty-free and ethically sourced ingredients matter, but clear evidence of efficacy is the primary purchase driver.
Retail sales associates who are knowledgeable increase trust and make conversion more likely.
Small lifestyle things (sleep, hydration, diet) contribute to skin appearance alongside topical treatments.
Hormonal fluctuations (e.g., stress, period) play a significant role in breakouts and pigmentation.
Product rituals (even when long) are therapeutic and help manage stress for her.
Good customer service and reasonable return policies influence loyalty to a retailer.
Beauty is a mix of self-expression and self-care—products should help both aesthetic and emotional goals.
Cultural beauty practices from her Korean heritage are meaningful and often more than just marketing buzz.
Over-exfoliation is common among Gen Z and can worsen hyperpigmentation if not managed properly.
Public reviews often reflect extremes; moderation and patterns across many reviewers are more informative.
Social media algorithms amplify certain brands and trends quickly, which can create unsustainable hype cycles.
Brands that invest in educational content (how to layer, order of ingredients) build long-term customer relationships.
It's reasonable to invest in a few core 'workhorse' products (sunscreen, vitamin C, gentle exfoliant, moisturiser) and supplement with fun, lower-cost items.
Peer recommendations (friend groups) are as influential as online influencers for day-to-day purchases.
Diversity in the beauty industry (representation of Asian creators, scientists, and execs) improves products and marketing that better suit her skin needs.
Not all skin problems require aggressive intervention—sometimes pausing actives and restoring the moisture barrier is the right move.
Cultural pride: being Asian American means blending modern Western regimens with family traditions around skincare, food, and self-presentation.
Mental health and skin health are intertwined—stress management indirectly benefits skin clarity and tone.
She prefers brands that respond fast and honestly to customer complaints and product reactions.
Learning to read ingredient lists is an act of self-advocacy in a complicated market.
skills: Intermediate ingredient literacy: understands hyaluronic acid, retinol, niacinamide, vitamin C (ascorbic acid, MAP), AHAs/BHAs/PHAs and basic pH implications.
Solid retail sales skills: product demonstrations, customer rapport, merchandising, and upselling with tact.
Basic skin analysis: can identify dryness, dehydration, mild sensitivity, PIH (post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation) versus erythema.
Multitasking under pressure during busy store shifts while maintaining friendly interaction.
Social research: able to synthesize consumer reviews, YouTube tests, and clinical claims to form purchase recommendations.
Budgeting: tracks spending, sets monthly beauty budgets, and times purchases for sales.
Content curation: takes clean photos, writes short reviews, and organizes product notes in a personal system.
Bilingual conversational ability in English and intermediate Korean—can communicate with family, read Korean product pages, and follow Korean-language videos.
Basic first-aid knowledge for mild skin reactions (stop product, cold compress, consult brand/customer service).
Customer service conflict-resolution: de-escalates tense situations and communicates store policy clearly.
Time management: balancing school, part-time work, and social life without missing critical deadlines.
Digital literacy: comfortable using POS systems, social platforms, spreadsheets (Google Sheets), and Canva for simple graphics.
Networking: building rapport with brand reps, colleagues, and beauty community contacts for early product knowledge.
Basic photographic styling of product flat-lays for social sharing and personal records.
Patch testing protocols and scheduling product introduction to minimize interactions between actives.

general: Maintains a detailed beauty journal with dates, photos, reaction notes and repurchase flags.
Watches a 20–30 minute YouTube review before making medium-to-high cost purchases.
Always applies sunscreen in the morning and restocks travel-size SPF in her bag for re-application after lunch or outdoor walks.
Uses sheet masks on Sundays or during low-energy study days as a self-care ritual.
Patch-tests new actives on inner arm or behind ear for 3–7 days before full-face application.
Trades deluxe samples with friends and rotates products to avoid single-use waste.
Takes product photos and writes mini-reviews in group chats after 1–2 weeks of use.
Returns or exchanges products promptly if a severe reaction occurs and documents the incident.
Prefers sequence: oil/gel cleanser (night), gentle foam cleanser (AM if needed), toner/essence, hydrating serum, targeted actives (every other night), moisturizer, sunscreen (AM).
Avoids combining high-potency retinol and strong AHA nights consecutively to reduce inflammation risk.
Browses product aisles during lunch breaks to 'window shop' and gather inspiration.
Schedules heavier exfoliation and peels on non-social weeks to avoid visible flaking before events.
Is careful about layering order: thinnest textures first, then creams and oils.
Keeps a small portable kit of essentials (sunscreen, blotting papers, hyaluronic mist) in her backpack.
Saves money by buying a full-size only after at least two trial runs or receiving a deluxe sample.
Uses white noise or calming playlists when performing her nighttime 8–10 step routine to make it feel restorative.
Shares product deals and codes with friends via group chat and sometimes coordinates bulk orders to split shipping.
Avoids products with heavy fragrances after a previous irritation episode.
Attends in-store launch nights or influencer pop-ups occasionally to test new products and meet creators in person.
Keeps receipts and documents batch numbers for higher-risk actives in case a recall or adverse reaction requires contact with a brand.

morning: 6:45–7:30 AM: wakes, gentle oil cleanse if wearing heavy night products else water splash.
Applies a hydrating toner/essence, a vitamin C serum or antioxidant (on non-retinol nights), light moisturizer, and broad-spectrum SPF (at least SPF 50 PA+++/BROAD).
Applies lightweight makeup (tinted sunscreen or cushion compact, cream blush, brow gel) for school or work.
Prepares a quick breakfast (overnight oats, fruit, or toast) and a black coffee or iced tea.
Checks messages, skim beauty news and YouTube alerts while commuting or before classes.
evening: 8:00–10:30 PM (varies with classes): double-cleanses if wearing makeup—oil cleanser followed by gentle foam.

Applies layered essences, hydrating serum with hyaluronic acid, targeted actives (e.g., retinol every third night, niacinamide daily), spot-treats active blemishes with benzoyl peroxide or sulfur patches, applies moisturizer, and occasionally a sleeping mask or oil.
Applies sheet mask 1–2x/week or before events; uses sleeping packs for extra occlusion during dry winter months.
Does a short journaling session to note any new sensitivities or improvements and to log product usage.
Prepares products for next day and sets sunscreen refill in bag.
weekend: Longer self-care sessions including exfoliating acids (AHA/PHA) on a scheduled night, longer sheet mask sessions, and some DIY treatments.
Visits specialty stores to sample new launches and attend occasional workshops.
Has brunch with friends and tries new cafes in the neighborhood.
Takes a longer walk or attends a yoga class in the morning.
Does a deeper clean of her product stash and organizes samples by priority.

skin.type: Normal / balanced skin (leans combination T-zone in summer)
skin.primary_concerns: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) from past acne, scattered dark spots and uneven skin tone, occasional closed comedones around chin and jawline, minimal redness from new actives if overused.
skin.routine_management: Uses sunscreen daily, vitamin C in the AM, niacinamide in PM, introduces retinol gradually (low concentration, every 3–4 nights), hydrates with hyaluronic acid and ceramide-rich moisturizers when barrier compromised.

general_physical.overall: Good general health, BMI in healthy range, active lifestyle (yoga, walking), no chronic illnesses.
general_physical.allergies: Seasonal pollen allergies (spring) with occasional antihistamine use; no known drug allergies.
general_physical.medications: Occasional OTC antihistamines for seasonal allergies; uses topical spot treatments as needed.
general_physical.vision_hearing: Wears non-prescription reading glasses occasionally; normal hearing.
general_physical.reproductive_health: Regular menstrual cycles; uses barrier methods or is single—no hormonal contraception currently but open to discussing with provider if acne management requires it.

mental_health.status: Generally stable with occasional anxiety spikes around exams and work scheduling. Practices journaling and breathing exercises. Has one episode of moderate stress-related insomnia during finals in the past year but no diagnosis of major depression or anxiety disorder. Open to counseling if stress becomes chronic.
mental_health.coping: Skincare routine functions as partial stress management. Uses social support from friends and family, yoga, and scheduled digital breaks.

medical_history: No hospitalizations. Minor childhood surgeries (appendectomy not required).
Past teenage acne treated with OTC benzoyl peroxide and topical retinoid cream (short course).

members.description: Korean immigrant, bilingual (Korean/English), works as a school administrative assistant. Encouraging about education and supportive of Hana's part-time work. Shares beauty traditions from Korea and occasionally gifts skincare products from family trips.
First-generation Korean American father, works in IT for a small company. Practical and cautious about spending. Encourages Hana to finish school and prioritize stable employment.
High-school sibling who sometimes borrows Hana's sheet masks and makeup. Close but occasionally competitive over grades and family attention.

relationships.status: Single but casually dating; prioritizes school and side projects over committed relationship currently. Comfortable with slow-moving dating and values emotional maturity.

members.description: Three close friends from university who share beauty interests; they exchange product finds, split shipping on online purchases, and attend pop-ups together.
A rotating group of colleagues at Sephora and the K-beauty boutique who trade insider tips, coordinate schedules, and test products together after shifts.
Active member of a small Discord/Telegram group of K-beauty enthusiasts where she posts product journals and reads others' experiences.
A mid-level brand rep who occasionally gives Hana previews, training materials, and advice on career paths in beauty marketing.
Undergraduate instructor who has encouraged Hana to consider an internship in market research; provides letter-of-recommendation support.

other_facts: Born and raised in Los Angeles to Korean immigrant parents; household bilingual with family dinners often in Korean.
First skincare memory: age 12, grandmother teaching gentle rice water rinses and layering light sunscreen before outdoor family events.
Started serious multi-step routines at 16 after watching K-beauty videos and transplanting those rituals into her daily life.
Transferred from a community college to UC system, proud of balancing work and academics to make the transition.
Keeps a dedicated 'trial shelf' in her room with 20–30 deluxe samples categorized by active (vitamin C, retinol, acids, hydrating).
Has an ongoing spreadsheet that logs product name, start date, reaction, cost per mL, and 'HG' status.

Favorite current products include a stable vitamin C serum in a pump, a niacinamide hybrid serum, a ceramide-rich night cream, and a non-greasy SPF 50 physical-chemical hybrid preferred for no white cast.
A recent viral impulse buy (a trending sleeping mask) didn't perform as advertised; she documented the mismatch in her group chat as a learning example.
Often photographs product textures (droplet photos of serums) to compare absorption rates over time.
Attended a K-beauty pop-up in DTLA last year where she met a favorite YouTuber and got a signed postcard—still framed at home.
Has a small jar of sheet mask essences saved to dampen a cotton pad for intensive neck hydration occasionally.
Enjoys visiting Korean supermarkets and often buys traditional snacks that remind her of home; associates certain foods with comfort and family gatherings.
Is exploring minimalist capsule wardrobes with a few statement pieces and plenty of thrifted items.
Has side hobbies: analog photography (disposable cameras at festivals), pottery basics classes at a weekend studio, and making Spotify playlists.
Has a soft spot for limited-edition packaging and keeps a few empty jars because they look pretty on her shelf.
Received her first digital camera from a summer job; uses it to document product flat-lays and campus life.
Volunteered once at a campus mental health awareness event and found the experience rewarding; considers future volunteer opportunities in student services.
Once had a mild allergic reaction to a fragrance-heavy serum (localized redness and itching); learned to be cautious with perfumed products afterwards.
Uses a humidifier during winter months to maintain skin barrier health and avoid flaking from retinoids.
Has a favorite small local cafe where baristas know her order and which she uses as a quiet study spot between classes.
Likes to plan skincare 'experiments'—one month focused on brightening, next month on barrier repair—and documents photographic results.
Has moderate social media engagement: posts monthly skincare updates and occasional haul videos; prefers being a micro-creator rather than full-time influencer.
Is pragmatic about returns: keeps original packaging and receipts in a labeled box for easy exchanges.
Values mentorship: saved notes from in-store brand trainings and revisits them when explaining products to customers.
Tries to limit screen time before bed but sometimes watches long-form YouTube which can delay sleep—she compensates with guided breathing when needed.
Enjoys hosting small 'mask and movie' nights with friends where everyone brings a sheet mask and a comfort snack.
Has a favorite dermatologist she sees once a year for a professional opinion on persistent PIH and to discuss stronger topical options if needed.
Collects travel-size versions of favorite products to simplify packing for weekend getaways.
Participated in a campus market research study on Gen Z beauty habits and enjoyed providing feedback; found the academic angle fulfilling.
Keeps a small emergency skin kit in her backpack (gentle cleanser wipes, sunscreen stick, hydrocolloid patches for sudden blemishes).
Strong memory of helping her mother choose products for a family wedding; that event shaped her interest in beauty as both practical and sentimental.
Has an anecdote of an early retail customer who returned a product upset—the interaction taught her the value of active listening and empathy in customer service.
Visited Seoul with family once as a teenager and was inspired by neighborhood beauty shops and multi-floor cosmetic stores; that trip cemented her interest in K-beauty.
Has a constructive habit of unsubscribing from brand emails that feel too spammy; keeps those from brands she loves and trusts.
Keeps a small fund for 'beauty emergency' purchases when something unexpectedly runs out (e.g., sunscreen) before payday.
Stores expiration dates on products in her phone calendar to avoid using compromised actives.
Is curious about a future pivot into brand-side roles (product developer liaison or retail manager) but wants to gain more professional experience first.
Once organized a small clothing swap with friends and included a beauty-sample exchange; it was a hit and she plans to repeat it.
Remembers being teased briefly in middle school for obsessive routines but matured into confidence and now mentors younger family members on gentle skincare.
Has a small relapse of impulse buying during major influencer hauls but uses a 48-hour cooling-off rule now to curb unnecessary purchases.
Values privacy; despite being active in online communities she rarely shares personal photos that reveal sensitive family or financial details.
Has an aspiration to someday curate a small, well-edited online store of tried-and-true K-beauty essentials, built from her testing spreadsheet and community feedback.
